title: practical english usage - Part 126
content:
We go out for a drink together every so often / every now and then.

every so often; every now and then We meet every other Tuesday. (= ... every second Tuesday.) every other She visits her mother every single day.

Note the following common expressions with every.

8  common       expressions You don't see elephants every day.

In everyday life, you don't often find an elephant in a supermarket.

as the adverbial expression every day. Compare: Everyday is an adjective meaning 'ordinary’ ’ , 'usual', 'routine'. It is not the same 7 everyday can refer to things as well as people).

Note that everyone (= ‘ everybody’ ) does not mean the same as every one (which When everybody had finished eating the waiters took away their plates.

Has everybody got their tickets? (less formal) Has everybody got his or her ticket? (more formal) a plural word makes sense. Compare: usually be either singular (more formal) or plural (less formal). Sometimes only When  possessives and pronouns refer back to everybody/everyone, they can / found that everywhere was closed.

Everything I like is either illegal, immoral or fattening.

Everybody has gone home, (     Everybody have...) like every.

Everybody, everyone, everything and everywhere are used with singular verbs, 6 everybody,           etc She had to stop and rest every two or three steps.

/ see her every few days.  There's a meeting every six weeks.

Every is used before a plural noun in expressions that refer to intervals.

5 every      + plural     noun cannot fly.) Not every kind of bird can fly. (More natural than Every kind of bird To  negate every, we  normally use not every.

4 negative       structures grammar »153     each I phone each of my children once a week.

Each of us sees the world differently.

my, these) with a plural noun.

We   use each of before a plural pronoun, or before a determiner (for example the, 2  each      of not Each new day is different, (     . . .arc different.) A  following verb is also singular.

I enjoy each moment, (     ... each moments!) Each is a determiner. We   use it before a singular noun.

1  each      + singular 153 each For the difference between every and each, ►     154.

She was here every day. (Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, ...) She was here all day. (= from morning to night) Note the difference between all day/week, etc and every day/week, etc.

4 all   day     and   every      day,    etc not I like all music, (     .. . every music.) We   can use all, but not every, with uncountable nouns.

3 all   with    uncountables not I’ ve written to every friend I have, (     ... every my friend Amy  every friend.) - I've written to all (of) my friends.

Every plate was broken, (     Every the plate / The every plate...) - All (of) the plates were broken.

possessives or demonstratives). Compare: We   can use all (of), but not normally every, with certain determiners (articles, 2  every      not   used    with    determiners All (of) the lights were out.

- Every light was out.

All children need love.

- Every child needs love, (     All child needs love.) plural noun. Compare: Every is used with a singular noun. To  give the same meaning, all is used with a 1  every      with    singular      nouns;     all   with    plurals structures.

often suggests 'without exception'. The  two words are used in different about all the members of a group. There is little difference of meaning; every Every and all can both be used to talk about people or things in general, or 152 every      and   all Determiners:       Quantifiers        Section  13 grammar •  154  each and every: the difference Every patient came from the same small village.

Each person in turn went to see the doctor.

together, in a group. (Every is closer to all.) So we  are more likely to say: time. And every is more common when we  are thinking of people or things But we  prefer each when we  are thinking of people or things separately, one at a You look more beautiful each/every time I see you.

Each and every can often be used without much difference of meaning.
